49.title: Day 49 Assignment: REST API Design Principles
49.description: Evaluate your practical and interview-ready understanding of rest api design principles through concepts, scenario reasoning, and coding execution.

questions.title: REST API Design Principles: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: Explain the key principles of **REST API Design Principles** and describe one common misunderstanding candidates make in interviews.
hints: Define the core terms in REST API Design Principles before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Core idea
REST API Design Principles should be explained as a **problem-to-solution** concept, not just syntax or framework keywords.

## What strong answers include
- Correct definition of the main mechanism.
- A realistic use case where the mechanism is preferable.
- One limitation/trade-off and mitigation.

## Common misunderstanding
Candidates often give API names only, but miss **why** this pattern exists and where it can fail under load or team scale.

## Interview framing
A concise answer usually follows: definition -> when to use -> pitfall -> trade-off.

questions.title: REST API Design Principles Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Given two ways to implement **REST API Design Principles**, how would you compare them for readability, performance, and operational risk?
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## Comparison method
Use a decision matrix: **correctness**, **complexity**, **performance**, **observability**, and **operational safety**.

## Practical rule
Pick the simplest option that meets non-functional requirements, then add complexity only when metrics justify it.

## What interviewers want
Evidence that you can reason beyond syntax and defend implementation choices with clear trade-offs.

questions.title: REST API Design Principles Interview Scenario
questions.question:
You are asked to solve this production-style situation related to **REST API Design Principles**: Design hotel booking API endpoints and status codes.

Describe your diagnosis approach, decisions, and safe rollout steps.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Step 1: Clarify impact
Define affected users, error rates, latency, data correctness risk, and whether partial degradation is acceptable.

## Step 2: Build a diagnosis plan
Use logs, metrics, traces, and targeted tests to separate root cause from side effects.

## Step 3: Apply the fix safely
- Use minimal-risk changes first.
- Add guards (timeouts, retries, validation, feature flags) where relevant.
- Verify with canary and objective success criteria.

## Step 4: Prevent recurrence
Create tests and runbook updates for this failure mode.

```text
Strong interview answers show both technical depth and operational judgment.
```

questions.title: REST API Design Principles Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **REST API Design Principles**:

Design hotel booking API endpoints and status codes.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput:
Topic: REST API Design Principles
Challenge solved: Design hotel booking API endpoints and status codes.
Day: 49

questions.title: REST API Design Principles Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A second-order problem appears after your initial solution in **REST API Design Principles**.

Using this context: Design hotel booking API endpoints and status codes.

How do you adapt design/operations safely?
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## Adaptation strategy
1. Re-state current invariants and what changed.
2. Keep backward compatibility where possible.
3. Introduce guardrails (rate limits, retries, feature flags, contracts).

## Decision framing
A strong senior answer balances delivery speed with failure containment.

## Verification
Define measurable criteria before and after rollout so the change is provably better.

questions.title: REST API Design Principles Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: List 4 interview keywords for **REST API Design Principles**, define each in one line, and mention one anti-pattern to avoid.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Crisp definitions
Provide precise definitions with practical framing (where it helps and where it can hurt).

## Anti-pattern
Name one misuse pattern and why it causes maintainability or reliability issues.

## Interview tip
Clarity beats jargon density — short, correct definitions score well.

56.title: Day 56 Assignment: Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions
56.description: Evaluate your practical and interview-ready understanding of saga pattern & distributed transactions through concepts, scenario reasoning, and coding execution.

questions.title: Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: Explain the key principles of **Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions** and describe one common misunderstanding candidates make in interviews.
hints: Define the core terms in Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Core idea
Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions should be explained as a **problem-to-solution** concept, not just syntax or framework keywords.

## What strong answers include
- Correct definition of the main mechanism.
- A realistic use case where the mechanism is preferable.
- One limitation/trade-off and mitigation.

## Common misunderstanding
Candidates often give API names only, but miss **why** this pattern exists and where it can fail under load or team scale.

## Interview framing
A concise answer usually follows: definition -> when to use -> pitfall -> trade-off.

questions.title: Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Given two ways to implement **Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions**, how would you compare them for readability, performance, and operational risk?
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## Comparison method
Use a decision matrix: **correctness**, **complexity**, **performance**, **observability**, and **operational safety**.

## Practical rule
Pick the simplest option that meets non-functional requirements, then add complexity only when metrics justify it.

## What interviewers want
Evidence that you can reason beyond syntax and defend implementation choices with clear trade-offs.

questions.title: Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions Interview Scenario
questions.question:
You are asked to solve this production-style situation related to **Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions**: Compensating payment after inventory failure.

Describe your diagnosis approach, decisions, and safe rollout steps.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Step 1: Clarify impact
Define affected users, error rates, latency, data correctness risk, and whether partial degradation is acceptable.

## Step 2: Build a diagnosis plan
Use logs, metrics, traces, and targeted tests to separate root cause from side effects.

## Step 3: Apply the fix safely
- Use minimal-risk changes first.
- Add guards (timeouts, retries, validation, feature flags) where relevant.
- Verify with canary and objective success criteria.

## Step 4: Prevent recurrence
Create tests and runbook updates for this failure mode.

```text
Strong interview answers show both technical depth and operational judgment.
```
